text: Beyond Blame: Rethinking SZZ with Knowledge Graph Search 3

• AgenticSZZ, the first approach to apply Temporal Knowledge Graphs to software evolution analysis, reframing BIC identification as a graph search problem and enabling identification of BICs beyond the blame-defined candidate set.
• Comprehensive evaluation demonstrating state-of-the-art performance, with ablation analysis validating the contribution of each component.

SZZ Algorithm and Its Variants. The SZZ algorithm [ 32 ] pioneered automated BIC identification by tracing deleted or modified lines in bug-fixing commits via git blame . Over two decades, numerous variants have improved precision through annotation graphs and cosmetic change filtering [ 19 ], meta-change exclusion [ 7 ], refactoring detection [ 24 , 25 ], deep learning [ 35 ], and LLM- based reasoning [ 36 , 40 ]. Most recently, LLM4SZZ [ 36 ] leverages LLMs to rank blame candidates and expands context around modified lines for candidate collection. We provide detailed descriptions of our baselines in Section 5.1 . Despite these advances, all approaches share a fundamental limitation: they restrict the search space to commits returned by git blame , inherently missing BICs that require broader history traversal. AgenticSZZ addresses this limitation by structuring the commit history as a temporal knowledge graph and navigating it through agent-guided exploration.

Knowledge Graphs (KGs) in Software Engineering (SE). A KG is a graph-based data structure where nodes represent entities and edges encode relationships between them, enabling queries that traverse and reason over these connections [ 16 ]. KGs have been increasingly applied to SE tasks. For automated program repair, repository-aware KGs bridge semantic gaps between issue descriptions and code patches [ 45 ], and unified code KGs enable multilingual issue resolution [ 5 ]. For bug localization, injecting KG-derived knowledge into LLMs improves fault localization accuracy [ 21 ]. Crash solution recommendation has been addressed by mining Stack Overflow into structured KGs [ 10 ]. However, none of these works address BIC identification or model the temporal evolution of commits. Temporal knowledge graphs (TKGs) extend KGs with time-aware reasoning, enabling prediction over evolving relationships [ 26 , 28 , 38 ]. To our knowledge, AgenticSZZ is the first approach to apply TKGs to software evolution analysis. The temporal dimension is essential for BIC identification: a commit can only induce a bug if it precedes the fix, and understanding causal relationships requires reasoning about the sequence of code changes over time.

LLM Agents for SE. LLM-based agents have emerged as powerful tools for complex SE tasks, marking a shift toward Agentic SE (SE 3.0) where agents work as AI teammates [ 14 , 20 ]. Recent ap- proaches navigate codebases autonomously to resolve GitHub issues [ 46 , 47 ], using tool-augmented LLMs to search, read, and edit files. Simpler pipelines without persistent agent state have also proven effective [ 43 ], general-purpose agent frameworks provide flexible foundations [ 41 ], and unified agents have been proposed to orchestrate multiple SE capabilities [ 1 ]. For bug fixing specifically, combining code KGs with agentic reasoning has shown promise [ 22 ]. An LLM-based framework has been proposed for BIC localization by integrating execution traces and commit artifacts [ 40 ]. However, this approach requires test execution for candidate filtering and does not model the tem- poral structure of commit history. In contrast, AgenticSZZ operates over a TKG of commit history, using specialized tools designed for BIC search. This design enables reasoning about historical code evolution rather than static code states.
3 Preliminary Study (RQ0): Where are Bug-Inducing Commits Located?

Existing SZZ techniques [ 7 , 19 , 24 , 32 ] predominantly assume that bug-inducing commits (BICs) can be identified by applying git blame on the deleted or modified lines in the BFC. This is based on
